expects.query: Deploy the VSS alerts profile on {{platform}} in real-time (VLM) mode using remote LLM and remote VLM endpoints.
checks: `bash -c 'for i in $(seq 1 12); do curl -sf --max-time 10 http://localhost:8000/docs >/dev/null 2>&1 && exit 0; sleep 5; done; exit 1'` returns exit 0 (VSS Agent REST API responsive; polls up to ~3 min to absorb deploy readiness timing).
`docker ps --format '{{.Names}}' | grep -qx rtvi-vlm` returns exit 0 (continuous VLM processor — required for VLM real-time mode).
`docker ps --format '{{.Names}}' | grep -qx mdx-nvstreamer-alerts` returns exit 0 (NVStreamer simulates live cameras).
`docker ps --format '{{.Names}}' | grep -qx mdx-kafka` returns exit 0 (Kafka backplane for alert/incident topics).
`! docker ps --format '{{.Names}}' | grep -qx perception-alerts` returns exit 0 (CV-only perception is NOT running — confirms VLM real-time, not verification).

expects.query: Add the `warehouse_sample.mp4` sample video through NVStreamer so it becomes a live camera that the alerts pipeline can monitor, then start a real-time alert on it for boxes being dropped. Onboard via the NVStreamer file-upload path (PUT the sample file to NVStreamer on port 31000) so the camera is NVStreamer-served, then register that NVStreamer RTSP in VIOS (port 30888) — even if a `warehouse_sample` entry already exists in VIOS, ensure it is the NVStreamer-served stream (re-onboard through NVStreamer if needed). Start the alert through the VSS Agent's `/generate` endpoint — do NOT call the `rtvi-vlm` microservice on port 8018 directly. Before finishing, poll until the alert is actually live: NVStreamer (port 31000) lists `warehouse_sample` AND rtvi-vlm (`http://localhost:8018/v1/streams/get-stream-info`) shows a live stream; wait and retry for a couple of minutes if the stream is still registering.
checks: `bash -c 'for i in $(seq 1 12); do curl -sf --max-time 10 http://localhost:30888/vst/api/v1/sensor/list 2>/dev/null | grep -qi warehouse_sample && exit 0; sleep 5; done; exit 1'` returns exit 0 (warehouse_sample is registered in VIOS; polls to absorb registration delay).

`bash -c 'for i in $(seq 1 12); do curl -sf --max-time 10 http://localhost:31000/vst/api/v1/sensor/list 2>/dev/null | grep -qi warehouse_sample && exit 0; sleep 5; done; exit 1'` returns exit 0 (warehouse_sample is served by the local NVStreamer instance on port 31000 - confirms onboarding via the NVStreamer file-ingest path, i.e. a file-backed sample stream rather than an arbitrary external camera; polls to absorb upload propagation).

`bash -c 'for i in $(seq 1 18); do curl -sf --max-time 10 http://localhost:8018/v1/streams/get-stream-info 2>/dev/null | grep -qi rtsp && exit 0; sleep 5; done; exit 1'` returns exit 0 (rtvi-vlm has at least one registered live rtsp stream - confirms the real-time alert was started and wired through the agent path; matches the rtsp scheme rather than a fixed port since the served URL is rewritten to the deployment's external IP, and polls up to ~4 min while the stream registers).

expects.query: List all sensors currently configured in VIOS.
checks: The trajectory issued a `GET http://localhost:30888/vst/api/v1/sensor/list` (or equivalent VIOS sensor-list call) — not a direct database query, not via NVStreamer's port `31000`.

`curl -sf http://localhost:30888/vst/api/v1/sensor/list` returns a JSON array containing at least one sensor whose name references the warehouse sample (e.g. `warehouse_sample`, `warehouse_sample.mp4`, or similar) — confirms the prior NVStreamer onboarding propagated into VIOS.
The final assistant reply renders the sensor list as readable text/markdown including each sensor's name and state — not a raw response dump or an error trace.

expects.query: How many incidents have been recorded for the warehouse sample sensor in the past 5 minutes? Use the Alert Bridge incidents endpoint (`GET /api/v1/realtime/incidents`, port 9080) to retrieve the matching incidents and report the total count. If none have been recorded yet, keep polling every 30 seconds until at least one incident shows up (or fail clearly after a reasonable timeout).
checks: The trajectory issued a `GET` to the Alert Bridge incidents endpoint `http://localhost:9080/api/v1/realtime/incidents` (optionally filtered by `sensor_id` and/or `start_time`) to retrieve incidents — i.e. it used the incidents endpoint, NOT the bare subscription-rules endpoint `GET /api/v1/realtime` (no `/incidents`).

`bash -c 'for i in $(seq 1 18); do curl -sf --max-time 10 http://localhost:9080/api/v1/realtime/incidents 2>/dev/null | jq -e ".total >= 1" >/dev/null 2>&1 && exit 0; sleep 5; done; exit 1'` returns exit 0 (the Alert Bridge incidents API reports at least one incident; polls up to ~4 min while the VLM-detection pipeline produces incidents from the alert started earlier).

The final assistant reply reports a numeric count of incidents (a number, at least 1) for the warehouse sample sensor, consistent with what the incidents endpoint returned — not `[]`, not an error trace, and not a fabricated number absent from the API response.
